evals.name: parent-child-composition
evals.prompt: I have a ProfileFeature child reducer and need to embed it in a SettingsFeature parent. The profile is always visible (not optional). Show the parent reducer with proper composition and how the parent reads child state.
evals.expected_output: Parent uses Scope(state: \.child, action: \.child) before its own Reduce block. Parent reads child state directly via state.profile.someProperty.

assertions.text: Uses Scope(state: \.profile, action: \.profile) to embed the child
Places Scope BEFORE the parent Reduce block in body
assertions.rationale: ReducerBuilder ordering — child runs first. Baseline often places after.

assertions.text: Parent reads child state directly (state.profile.property) without actions
assertions.rationale: Parent can read child state, child cannot modify parent

assertions.text: Uses Reduce<State, Action> with explicit generics (not bare Reduce)
assertions.rationale: Explicit generics fix Xcode autocomplete issues

evals.name: long-running-effects-lifecycle
evals.prompt: Build a feature with a WebSocket connection that starts on appear and must be properly cancelled on disappear. The feature is presented modally via @Presents/ifLet. What happens to the WebSocket when the modal is dismissed? How would you handle an effect that needs to SURVIVE the modal being dismissed?
evals.expected_output: WebSocket uses .run with for try await + .cancellable(id:). When @Presents/ifLet dismisses the child, effects auto-cancel. For surviving effects, fire from the parent before dismissing.

assertions.text: WebSocket uses .run { send in for try await message in stream } pattern
Effect is marked .cancellable(id:) for cleanup
Explains that ifLet/Presents auto-cancels child effects on dismissal
assertions.rationale: Automatic cancellation on navigation teardown — key TCA lifecycle feature

assertions.text: For surviving effects: recommends firing from parent, not child
assertions.rationale: Effects that must survive child dismissal must be owned by parent

assertions.text: Notes that .cancel(id:) does NOT work across different reducer scopes

evals.name: dependency-client-basic
evals.prompt: Create a UserClient dependency for TCA that can fetch a user profile and update user settings. Show the client, registration, and usage in a reducer.
evals.expected_output: @DependencyClient struct with closure properties, DependencyKey with liveValue, DependencyValues extension, @Dependency usage in reducer.

assertions.text: Uses @DependencyClient macro on the struct (not a protocol)
assertions.rationale: Struct-of-closures is TCA's prescribed pattern — baseline often uses protocols

assertions.text: Implements DependencyKey with static liveValue
Extends DependencyValues with computed property (get/set)
Uses @Dependency(\.userClient) in the reducer

evals.name: dependency-resolution-timing
evals.prompt: I'm building a dependency client where one dependency (UserClient) needs to use another dependency (APIClient) internally. Show the correct way to compose dependencies. Also, my test is failing because the app entry point creates a store — how do I fix that?
evals.expected_output: Use @Dependency inside liveValue closures (resolved at call time), NOT captured at static init time. Guard app entry with _XCTIsTesting.

assertions.text: Uses @Dependency(\.apiClient) INSIDE the closure (resolved at call time)
assertions.rationale: Static init capture freezes dependency forever — key gotcha

assertions.text: Does NOT capture DependencyValues._current at static init time
Guards app entry with if !_XCTIsTesting { RootView(...) }
assertions.rationale: Prevents test host from creating production stores

assertions.text: Mentions unimplemented(placeholder:) for non-Void test returns

evals.name: tree-based-sheet
evals.prompt: Add a sheet presentation to a TCA feature. When the user taps 'Add Item', a form sheet should appear. When the form saves, the parent should receive the new item and dismiss the sheet.
evals.expected_output: @Presents destination, PresentationAction, ifLet, sheet(item: $store.scope(...)), delegate action for save.

assertions.text: Uses @Presents var destination (not @PresentationState)
Uses PresentationAction<Destination.Action> in parent Action
Uses .ifLet(\.$destination, action: \.destination) with $ prefix on key path
assertions.rationale: $ prefix required for @Presents projected value — easy to forget

assertions.text: View uses sheet(item: $store.scope(...)) not sheet(store:)
Dismisses by setting state.destination = nil after receiving delegate action
Uses @Reducer enum Destination for the sheet destination type (not a standalone @Presents per presentation)
assertions.rationale: Scalable pattern — baseline often uses direct @Presents var addItem which doesn't scale to multiple presentations

evals.name: stack-based-navigation
evals.prompt: Build a master-detail NavigationStack in TCA. A list screen should push to a detail screen, which can push to an edit screen. Support deep linking by constructing the path from a URL.
evals.expected_output: StackState/StackAction with @Reducer enum Path, forEach, NavigationStack(path: $store.scope(...)), deep linking via path.append.

assertions.text: Uses StackState<Path.State> and StackAction<Path.State, Path.Action>
Uses @Reducer enum for Path (not manual Destination struct)
Uses .forEach(\.path, action: \.path) in reducer body
View uses NavigationStack(path: $store.scope(state: \.path, action: \.path))
Deep linking constructs path via state.path.append() calls
assertions.rationale: Stack-based deep linking is easy — just construct the array

evals.name: navigation-anti-patterns
evals.expected_output: NavigationStack wrapping TabView is wrong — each tab needs its own stack. Single destination enum for independent presentations means they're mutually exclusive. Separate @Presents properties for concurrent presentations.

assertions.text: Identifies NavigationStack wrapping TabView as the cause of back-button issues
assertions.rationale: NavigationStacks cannot be nested — each tab owns its own

assertions.text: Recommends NavigationStack inside each tab view, not wrapping TabView
assertions.rationale: Each tab owns its own StackState — key architecture rule

assertions.text: Explains single Destination enum makes presentations mutually exclusive
Recommends separate @Presents properties for concurrent presentations
assertions.rationale: Separate @Presents for alert, sheet, confirmation dialog

assertions.text: Does NOT recommend using NavigationLink(state:) from child modules
assertions.rationale: Creates circular dependency on root Path type

evals.name: testclock-timer-test
evals.prompt: Write a test for a feature with a 30-second auto-refresh timer. The test should verify the timer fires, verify it can be cancelled by leaving the screen, and should run fast in CI (not wait 30 real seconds).
evals.expected_output: TestClock for precise control, clock.advance(by:) to skip time, task.cancel() for cleanup, ImmediateClock for CI speed.

assertions.text: Uses TestClock (not ImmediateClock) for the timer test to control time precisely
Uses clock.advance(by: .seconds(30)) to trigger the timer
assertions.rationale: Correct TestClock API for skipping time

assertions.text: Properly cancels the long-running timer effect in tests (via task.cancel(), .cancel(id:), or sending a cleanup action like onDisappear)
assertions.rationale: Without cancellation, test hangs waiting for infinite timer effect

assertions.text: Uses event-based action naming (e.g., timerTicked, onAppear, onDisappear) not imperative names (e.g., startTimer, incrementCount)
assertions.rationale: TCA convention: actions describe events that happened, not commands to execute

assertions.text: Timer effect uses for await _ in clock.timer(interval:) pattern with @Dependency(\.continuousClock)
assertions.rationale: Testable timer pattern — enables TestClock.advance to control time deterministically

evals.name: non-exhaustive-integration
evals.prompt: Write an integration test that verifies the full login flow: user enters credentials, taps login, the LoginFeature delegates to AppFeature which switches to the main tab and starts loading the feed. This crosses multiple feature boundaries.
evals.expected_output: Non-exhaustive TestStore with .off, sends login action, receives delegate, asserts only important state changes, skips intermediate mutations.

assertions.text: Sets store.exhaustivity = .off for the integration test
assertions.rationale: Exhaustive mode would be 100+ lines for multi-feature flows

assertions.text: Uses store.receive(\.login.delegate.didLogin) with nested key paths
assertions.rationale: Integration tests receive delegate actions across features

assertions.text: Asserts only the important state changes (selectedTab), not every intermediate mutation
Overrides all dependencies accessed by both parent and child features
assertions.rationale: Integration tests touch multiple dependency boundaries

evals.name: viewstore-to-modern
evals.expected_output: Removes all WithViewStore, NavigationStackStore, ForEachStore, CaseLet. Uses direct store access, NavigationStack(path:), ForEach, switch store.case.

assertions.text: Removes all WithViewStore wrappers
Replaces NavigationStackStore with NavigationStack(path: $store.scope(...))
Replaces ForEachStore with ForEach(store.scope(state: \.items, action: \.items))
Replaces CaseLet with switch store.case pattern
assertions.rationale: Modern destination rendering — CaseLet is deprecated

assertions.text: Uses @Bindable var store for $store bindings
Uses key path scope syntax (\.path, \.items) not closure-based scoping

evals.name: ios16-perception-tracking
evals.prompt: I'm migrating my TCA app to @ObservableState but I still need to support iOS 16. After adding @ObservableState, my views stopped updating. No compiler errors, just silent failures. What's wrong and how do I fix it?
evals.expected_output: iOS 16 requires WithPerceptionTracking wrapper. Without it, @ObservableState observation silently fails. Wrap view body contents in WithPerceptionTracking { }.

assertions.text: Identifies that iOS 16 requires WithPerceptionTracking for @ObservableState
assertions.rationale: No compile-time enforcement — silent failure is a major gotcha

assertions.text: Shows correct usage: WithPerceptionTracking { /* view content */ }
Explains this is NOT needed for iOS 17+ (native Observation framework)
assertions.rationale: Context for when to remove the wrapper

assertions.text: Warns that forgetting WithPerceptionTracking causes runtime warning but silent observation failure
assertions.rationale: The runtime warning is easy to miss in logs

assertions.text: Explains that child/extracted views also need their own WithPerceptionTracking wrapper
assertions.rationale: Each view body that reads @ObservableState must be wrapped independently

assertions.text: Shows @Perception.Bindable for creating $ bindings with @ObservableState on iOS 16
assertions.rationale: @Bindable is iOS 17+ only — iOS 16 needs the Perception backport

evals.name: ai-code-review
evals.expected_output: Fix all issues: add @Reducer macro, move State/Action inside struct, add @ObservableState, remove Equatable from Action, replace EffectTask.task with .run, replace WithViewStore with direct access, replace singleton with @DependencyClient, rename fetchData to fetchButtonTapped.

assertions.text: Adds @Reducer macro to the feature struct
Moves State and Action INSIDE the @Reducer struct
Adds @ObservableState to State
Removes Equatable from Action
Replaces EffectTask.task with .run { send in }
Replaces WithViewStore with direct store access
Replaces APIClient.shared singleton with @DependencyClient
Renames fetchData to event-based name like fetchButtonTapped

evals.name: performance-optimization
evals.prompt: My TCA app stutters when scrolling a list of 500 items. Each scroll position change sends an action. Also, I have a computed property on State that filters the list — it's called in the scope function. How do I diagnose and fix these performance issues?
evals.expected_output: High-frequency scroll actions overwhelm the reducer (~6ms per action). Move scroll tracking to @State in View. Pre-compute filtered list in reducer, don't compute in scope. Use _printChanges() and .signpost() for diagnosis.

assertions.text: Identifies ~6ms per action cost and its impact on 60fps (37.5% of frame budget)
assertions.rationale: Real-world production data from Arc Browser — specific number from skill

assertions.text: Recommends moving scroll tracking to @State in the view (not TCA state)
assertions.rationale: Not everything needs TCA state — UI-only state belongs in View

assertions.text: Identifies computed property in scope as O(n) per action (must be O(1))
assertions.rationale: Scope functions run for every action sent to the system

assertions.text: Recommends pre-computing filtered list in reducer, storing in state
assertions.rationale: Move computation to mutation time, not observation time

assertions.text: Mentions _printChanges() or .signpost() as diagnostic tools

evals.name: god-reducer-decomposition
evals.prompt: I have a god reducer that handles home, settings, profile, and notifications. It's 1200 lines. Home uses NavigationStack for drill-down, settings is a modal sheet, profile and notifications are tab siblings. Decompose it with proper navigation.
evals.expected_output: Separate @Reducer per feature. Scope for always-present tabs. @Presents/ifLet for settings sheet. StackState/forEach for home navigation. @Reducer enum for Path/Destination. Delegate actions. No nested NavigationStack.

assertions.text: Creates separate @Reducer struct for each feature (Home, Settings, Profile, Notifications)
Settings uses @Presents with .ifLet for modal presentation
Home uses StackState/StackAction with .forEach for NavigationStack
Each tab owns its own navigation — no shared StackState across tabs
Child-to-parent communication uses delegate actions
assertions.rationale: Baseline often lets parent observe internal child actions

assertions.text: Uses @Reducer enum for Path/Destination reducers
NavigationStack is inside each tab view, not wrapping TabView

evals.name: effect-state-capture
evals.expected_output: Two issues: (1) Capturing whole @ObservableState [state] captures hidden registrar — extract only needed values. (2) Using .send(.loadData) for code reuse causes unnecessary action traversal — use a helper function instead.

assertions.text: Identifies [state] capture of @ObservableState as dangerous (captures registrar)
assertions.rationale: Reference type inside value type — main-actor violations

assertions.text: Fixes by extracting needed values: let form = state.form
Identifies .send(.loadData) as anti-pattern for code reuse
assertions.rationale: Each sent action traverses entire reducer tree (~6ms in large apps)

assertions.text: Recommends helper function or mutating func on State instead

evals.name: mvvm-to-tca-refactor
evals.expected_output: Full TCA refactor with @Reducer, @ObservableState, @DependencyClient for API, IdentifiedArrayOf, proper effect patterns, and direct store access in the view.

assertions.text: Replaces ObservableObject/StateObject with @Reducer and @ObservableState
Replaces APIService.shared singleton with @DependencyClient struct-of-closures
Uses IdentifiedArrayOf<Product> instead of plain [Product]
assertions.rationale: TCA collection management — enables forEach composition

assertions.text: Pre-computes filteredProducts in reducer (stored property) instead of computed property in State
assertions.rationale: Computed properties in State re-evaluate on every scope call

assertions.text: View uses direct store access (no @StateObject, no WithViewStore)
Error handling uses .run { } catch: { } pattern with explicit error actions
Delete effect does NOT silently swallow errors (try? removed)
assertions.rationale: Original code silently ignores delete failures

evals.name: pre-1.0-environment-to-modern
evals.expected_output: Full modernization: @Reducer macro, @ObservableState, @Dependency replacing Environment, .run replacing Combine effects, no WithViewStore.

assertions.text: Replaces Reducer<State, Action, Environment> with @Reducer struct containing nested State/Action
Replaces Environment with @Dependency for API client and clock
assertions.rationale: Dependency injection system completely changed in TCA 1.0

assertions.text: Replaces Combine .catchToEffect with .run { send in } async/await
assertions.rationale: Combine effects are obsolete — .run is the modern API

assertions.text: Replaces AnySchedulerOf<DispatchQueue> with @Dependency(\.continuousClock)
assertions.rationale: Combine schedulers replaced by Clock protocol

assertions.text: Removes Equatable from Action enum
State/Action defined inside @Reducer struct body, not standalone types
Removes WithViewStore — uses direct store access with @Bindable or let store
Adds @ObservableState to State

evals.name: observableobject-to-observablestate
evals.expected_output: TCA reducer with @ObservableState, timer via @Dependency(\.continuousClock) with for await loop, proper cancellation on stop/disappear, history managed in state.

assertions.text: Replaces ObservableObject + @Published with @Reducer struct and @ObservableState State
Replaces Timer.publish Combine pipeline with @Dependency(\.continuousClock) and for await clock.timer()
Uses .cancellable(id:) for the timer effect with enum CancelID (not empty enum)
assertions.rationale: Proper effect lifecycle management with non-empty cancel ID

assertions.text: Cancels timer on both stopTimer action AND onDisappear
View removes @StateObject — uses let store or @Bindable var store
No weak self, no AnyCancellable — pure value-type state + effect cancellation
assertions.rationale: TCA eliminates reference-type memory management entirely

evals.name: combine-effects-to-modern
evals.expected_output: Modern async/await effects: .run with clock.sleep for debounce, .run with try/catch, inline sequential logic, .run { _ in } for fire-and-forget, enum CancelID.

assertions.text: Replaces Effect(value:).debounce with clock.sleep + .cancellable(cancelInFlight: true)
assertions.rationale: Combine debounce → async sleep + cancel-in-flight pattern

assertions.text: Replaces .eraseToEffect() / .map / .catch chain with .run { send in } and try/catch
Replaces Effect.concatenate of Effect(value:) with either inline .run or .concatenate(.send, .run, .send) pattern
assertions.rationale: Both inline sequential and .concatenate with .send are valid modern TCA patterns

assertions.text: Replaces Effect.fireAndForget with .run { _ in }
Replaces struct SearchCancelID: Hashable with enum CancelID { case search }
assertions.rationale: Empty structs also pruned in release builds — same as empty enums

assertions.text: Replaces environment.mainQueue with @Dependency(\.continuousClock)
Defines search/API dependencies using @DependencyClient struct-of-closures (not a free variable or global reference)
assertions.rationale: Complete migration requires proper DI — baseline often leaves dependencies as uninjected references

evals.name: extract-child-from-god-reducer
evals.expected_output: SettingsFeature extracted as separate @Reducer with own State/Action/dependencies. AppFeature composes via Scope. Logout communicated via delegate action. Alert uses AlertState.

assertions.text: Extracts a separate @Reducer struct SettingsFeature with its own State and Action
assertions.rationale: Core decomposition — each feature is independently testable

assertions.text: Uses delegate action for logout (child sends .delegate(.didLogout), parent handles it)
assertions.rationale: Child should not know about app-level reset logic

assertions.text: Parent composes child via Scope(state: \.settings, action: \.settings)
Replaces Bool showLogoutAlert with AlertState<Action> or @Presents alert
assertions.rationale: Type-safe alerts instead of raw Bool + .alert modifier

assertions.text: Settings dependencies (@Dependency) live in SettingsFeature, not AppFeature
assertions.rationale: Dependencies follow the feature that uses them

assertions.text: Parent only observes .settings(.delegate(...)) actions, not internal settings actions
Three-category action pattern (view/internal/delegate) in SettingsFeature
assertions.rationale: Best practice for any non-trivial feature
